text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Melanoma Program

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
REFERRING PHYSICIAN: [Primary Care Physician]
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, surveillance/treatment decision

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Renata Okonkwo is a 56-year-old female with metastatic melanoma (Stage IV M1c, BRAF V600E+) presenting today for review of recent PET-CT results and treatment decision-making regarding immunotherapy duration. She has been on nivolumab maintenance for approximately 23 months (27 months total immunotherapy). Today represents a landmark visit: her most recent PET-CT demonstrated complete metabolic response.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Okonkwo was originally diagnosed with Stage IV melanoma arising from a dysplastic nevus on the mid-back, with initial metastatic disease to bilateral axillary and inguinal lymph nodes, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and six hepatic lesions. BRAF V600E mutation was confirmed on Foundation One CDx at diagnosis; the NGS profile was notably complex, including concurrent PTEN loss, NF1 splice site mutation, and TP53 R248W, with a high TMB of 28 mut/Mb.

She received first-line dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, with a rapid and deep initial response. However, she progressed at approximately 8 months, with ctDNA demonstrating an acquired MAP2K1 C121S resistance mutation and BRAF amplification. She was transitioned to second-line ipilimumab (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg every 3 weeks. She completed 3 of 4 planned cycles before being hospitalized with Grade 3 immune-related colitis requiring IV methylprednisolone and infliximab. Ipilimumab was permanently discontinued. Following steroid taper and colitis resolution, she resumed nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance, which she has now been on for approximately 23 months.

Her response to immunotherapy has been progressive and durable. Initial restaging at 14 weeks showed a mixed but favorable partial response with LDH trending down from 389 to 241 U/L. Subsequent imaging has documented deepening responses — by 6 months on maintenance she had achieved approximately 65% reduction from immunotherapy baseline, and at 21 months on maintenance she was approaching near-complete response by CT criteria (right axillary node 0.5 cm, single residual hepatic lesion 0.3 cm). At her 24-month immunotherapy milestone visit she had a thorough discussion about discontinuation versus continuation; given the residual 0.3 cm hepatic lesion that was not quite meeting CT criteria for complete response, and with patient preference to pursue complete response before stopping, nivolumab was continued.

She underwent PET-CT (whole body FDG) with concurrent MRI brain three to four weeks ago. That study demonstrated complete metabolic response — no FDG-avid lesions identified, with the residual hepatic hypodensity showing SUVmax below background liver activity, consistent with fibrotic scar. LDH came back at 131 U/L (normal). Brain MRI: no intracranial metastases.

Today she returns to discuss these results and finalize the decision regarding nivolumab discontinuation.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: No fevers, chills, or night sweats. Energy excellent. Weight stable (patient reports she has actually regained approximately 4 lbs she lost during the colitis hospitalization two years ago, and maintains a healthy weight).
Skin: No new rashes, lesions, or pigmented lesions of concern noted by patient or at most recent dermatology visit approximately 4 weeks ago (clear). No recurrence of squamoproliferative lesions (had two SCCs excised during dabrafenib therapy, none since switching to immunotherapy).
Gastrointestinal: Bowel habits at personal baseline, 1-2 formed stools per day. No diarrhea, abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or urgency. She notes this is a significant improvement from the colitis episode and she is grateful for full GI recovery.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Mild cold intolerance, stable, consistent with known hypothyroidism. On levothyroxine 112 mcg daily; last TSH was 2.8 mIU/L (within normal range). No symptoms of thyrotoxicosis. No adrenal insufficiency symptoms (no excessive fatigue, orthostatic symptoms, salt craving).
Musculoskeletal: No new arthralgias or myalgias beyond mild chronic aching she attributes to her level of physical activity.
Neurologic: No headaches, visual disturbances, focal deficits, or seizures. No cognitive changes.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or chest pain. No symptoms suggestive of pneumonitis.
Ophthalmologic: No new visual symptoms. She has a history of anterior uveitis (remote, predating melanoma diagnosis); last ophthalmology visit was clear.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or edema (no proteinuria on recent urinalysis).

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, temperature 36.9°C. Height 165 cm, weight 62 kg, BSA 1.67 m², BMI 22.8 kg/m².
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no distress. Mood upbeat and animated.
Skin: No active rash. Well-healed excision scars on the left forearm and right shin (prior SCC excision sites). Mid-back: well-healed scar at primary melanoma excision site, no ulceration, no satellite lesions, no local recurrence clinically. Multiple atypical nevi on trunk, unchanged from prior exam per patient. No new suspicious pigmented lesions appreciated on gross skin survey (comprehensive dermatologic exam performed 4 weeks ago — clear).
Lymph nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy. Bilateral axillary regions soft and non-tender without discrete nodularity — a marked and meaningful change from presentation when the right axillary node was 4.2 cm and grossly palpable.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly palpated. No peritoneal signs. Well-healed abdominal wall scar from prior biopsy site.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No pleural rub.
Neurologic: Alert and oriented x4. No focal deficits. Cranial nerves intact. Gait normal.
Extremities: No edema. Peripheral pulses intact.

LABORATORY RESULTS (from today's visit and recent draws)
CBC: WBC 6.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 58%, lymphocytes 32%, monocytes 8%), Hgb 13.4 g/dL, platelets 231 × 10³/µL.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 140, K 4.0, CO2 26, BUN 14, Cr 0.87 mg/dL (eGFR >60). AST 26 U/L, ALT 29 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 72 U/L. Albumin 4.2 g/dL, total protein 7.1 g/dL, glucose 91 mg/dL.
LDH: 131 U/L (normal; had been 312 U/L at initial diagnosis, peaked 389 U/L at progression on BRAF/MEK therapy).
Thyroid function: TSH 2.8 mIU/L (normal on levothyroxine 112 mcg). Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal).
Urinalysis: No proteinuria, no hematuria, no casts. (Monitoring for immune-related nephritis.)
No cortisol level ordered today; no clinical concern for immune-related adrenal insufficiency.

IMAGING RESULTS
PET-CT (whole body FDG with concurrent MRI brain, reviewed from approximately 3-4 weeks ago):
- No FDG-avid lesions identified anywhere in the body.
- Residual hepatic hypodensity (segment 6, 0.3 cm): SUVmax below background liver activity. Appearances entirely consistent with fibrotic scar/burn-out lesion with no metabolically active tumor.
- Bilateral axillary regions: no FDG-avid adenopathy; nodes well within normal size limits.
- No subcutaneous, peritoneal, nodal, or bone disease.
- MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Unremarkable study.
- LDH 131 U/L at time of scan.
Impression: Complete metabolic response. No evidence of active disease.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV M1c BRAF V600E+ malignant melanoma who has achieved complete metabolic response after a prolonged course of immunotherapy. This is a remarkable outcome given her initial disease burden (M1c with hepatic, peritoneal, nodal, and subcutaneous involvement, LDH 1.5x ULN, high-risk genomic profile with PTEN loss, NF1 mutation, and TP53 mutation).

1. MELANOMA — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE, TREATMENT DISCONTINUATION:
After extensive discussion today that the patient had clearly thought through carefully in advance, I am recommending discontinuation of nivolumab. This is the appropriate decision at this juncture for the following reasons: (a) she has achieved PET-confirmed complete metabolic response, (b) she has now completed 24 months of PD-1 monotherapy maintenance (27 months total immunotherapy), and (c) continuing beyond confirmed complete response without additional evidence of benefit is not supported by the available data and carries ongoing cumulative toxicity risk. The data from CheckMate-067 long-term follow-up and from the KEYNOTE-006 experience suggest that patients who achieve complete response and discontinue treatment can have durable outcomes, though relapse remains possible in approximately 25-35% at 2 years. I reviewed the relapse risk with her in detail. She understands that if relapse occurs, re-induction with PD-1-based therapy or potentially combination immunotherapy is likely manageable, though retreatment after prior ipilimumab and colitis would require careful consideration (ipi re-challenge would be avoided given prior Grade 3 GI toxicity). She understands and accepts these considerations. Last nivolumab infusion administered today — Opdivo 480 mg IV over 30 minutes, without incident.

2. SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: every 3 months for 2 years, then every 6 months thereafter (per NCCN and institutional protocol for melanoma off treatment after complete response)
   - MRI brain with gadolinium: every 6 months for 2 years (continued brain surveillance given M1c disease history, even without prior brain mets)
   - Dermatology: every 4 months (increased frequency given history of cutaneous SCCs on BRAF inhibitor and ongoing melanoma surveillance)
   - LDH quarterly
   - CBC and CMP every 3 months for first year off treatment to monitor for delayed immune-related toxicities
   - TSH every 3-4 months (ongoing monitoring for hypothyroidism, which may require dose adjustment independent of immunotherapy)

3. IMMUNE-RELATED ADVERSE EVENT MONITORING (OFF TREATMENT):
Patients can develop immune-related adverse events even after immunotherapy discontinuation, sometimes weeks to months later. The patient is counseled to report any of the following immediately: diarrhea more than 3 stools above baseline per day, abdominal pain, skin rash, shortness of breath, cough, jaundice, vision changes, significant fatigue, or any new symptoms. She has a clear understanding of her prior colitis and the importance of early identification. Gastroenterology has cleared her from active follow-up (colitis in full sustained remission). She retains GI contact information in case of flare after discontinuation.

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
Continue levothyroxine 112 mcg daily. TSH was 2.8 mIU/L — well-controlled. Endocrinology follow-up ongoing. This will likely be a permanent condition regardless of immunotherapy status; immune-mediated thyroiditis following checkpoint inhibition rarely reverses. Patient is established with endocrinology.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Okonkwo is doing exceptionally well emotionally. She describes returning fully to her career as a landscape architect, engaging in her community, and maintaining healthy habits including regular walking and yoga. She tearfully expressed gratitude today. I reminded her that while we are celebrating an extraordinary result, the importance of surveillance cannot be overstated and she should call with any new symptoms or concerns without delay. I emphasized we will remain her medical home for long-term follow-up. She has support from her partner and family. Referral to cancer survivorship program discussed and patient interested — referral placed today.

6. RETURN PLAN:
First post-treatment surveillance visit in approximately 3 months with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and lab work. MRI brain to be scheduled at 6 months post-discontinuation. Patient to contact clinic if any new symptoms arise in the interim.

Total visit time: approximately 60 minutes (including extended counseling regarding treatment discontinuation and surveillance)

Electronically signed: [Attending Medical Oncologist, MD]
Co-signed: [Fellow/Advanced Practice Provider]
Copies: Dermatology, Endocrinology, Gastroenterology, Primary Care
